So you were here for two months for free, now you've got to start paying, right? So the story would be the person who left, and then, you know, two years later came back, was drug backed by somebody, revolutionized their business, and during those two years, all the pain and suffering they endured, it wouldn't have been necessary if they just stuck around for a little longer to get it. You need some sort of story to get them over that, to get them over that hump, right? And or specific testimonials who tell that story. And that's a well-sold story too, to go to the guys who have left. Okay, good. Okay. Page 84, principle number 10 is principle of multi-purposing. And this is also important for being fast and being prolific. So pandering to what you guys want, this is an important principle. And that is that the same story can be used to make many different points. So most stories aren't limited to one purpose. A good story may be used on Tuesday for purpose A, and used two weeks from Thursday for purpose B. And so you want to think about how you can use it in different ways. Well, like I really like that I used the Kenneth Cole story from time to time of him wanting to exhibit and park this big van in front of the shoe show and the city telling him, you can't get a permit for that. Well, what can you get a permit for? Well, the only thing you can get a permit for is if you're going to shoot a movie or a TV show. So he immediately goes and changes his company name to Kenneth Cole Productions and now parks the van there and hires some cameraman to stand around with cameras and lights and bullies and business. So there's not that story. I've used it three ways, right? One is to do whatever it takes thing. The other is never take the first no for an answer, right? There's a second way to ask the question, a third way to ask the question, a fourth way to ask the question. I use the eyes near climbing over the fence to see what the competitors are doing. Trump has a foreclosure story that has at least two purposes. So you want to think about multi-purposing and there's slightly different ways to tell the same story that might get you to a different point. So if you look on page 85, here's a sample story. Take a minute to read the story. Let me know when you're done. I can't judge other people's reading speed. Okay. Flip the page. I'll give you two. So how many different ways can we use the story? What little different bridges can we write at the end to get us to a different place? So one is we can use it to talk about the problem with giving customers too many choices. So the last thing you want to do is sales person give them so many choices that they sit there and get eight. So we can use the story as a sales training story. We can also use the story about the paralysis of analysis. And so we can use it as a business management story. We can use it as a paralysis of analysis versus decision and action story to pitch somebody on taking immediate action rather than mulling things over. What are some of the other ways we might be able to use the same story? Who sees a way they could use it in their business specifically or a generic way that the same story can be used differently?
